chunk_summary: Defines stunting as HAZ < -2, outlines three action levels for management, and emphasizes the need for planned follow-up cadence to address chronic growth constraints in children under 5 years.
learning_objectives: Understand the definition of stunting and its clinical implications.
Differentiate between the three action levels of stunting and their follow-up protocols.
Recognize the importance of early referral for stunting with red flags for pathology.
Learn the role of WASH and micronutrient interventions in managing stunting.
key_facts: Stunting is defined as height-for-age z-score (HAZ) < -2 on WHO Child Growth Standards for children aged 0–5 years.
Stunting is associated with adverse neurodevelopment and long-term human-capital losses.
Action Level 1: Uncomplicated stunting (HAZ < -2, no danger signs); follow-up monthly, then every 3 months after improvement.
Action Level 2: Stunting with recurrent morbidity or anemia phenotype; follow-up every 2–4 weeks, then monthly after stabilization.
Action Level 3: Stunting with red flags (e.g., poor growth velocity, dysmorphism, developmental delay); initiate tiered evaluation and early referral.
Linear growth responds slowly, requiring planned follow-up cadence rather than acute 'treat and exit' approaches.
reference_quote: Stunting is defined as height-for-age z-score (HAZ) < -2 on the WHO Child Growth Standards (0–5 years).
document_key_topics: Definition and clinical implications of stunting
Early referral for stunting with red flags
